title: Chicken vindaloo
subtitle: Chicken in a vinegar and chilli paste, Goan style
excerpt: Boneless chicken thighs marinated in a vinegar-based paste of dried chillies, garlic, and warm spices, then braised until the sauce is thick and deeply flavoured. The same vindaloo method as pork, with chicken's shorter cook time making this a quicker weeknight version.
sourceNotes: Based on Goan vindaloo technique documented in Floyd Cardoz, 'Floyd Cardoz: Flavorwalla' (2016) and Madhur Jaffrey, 'Madhur Jaffrey's Indian Cooking' (1983). Chicken vindaloo is a later variant of the original pork dish, adapted for households and communities that do not eat pork.

content.text: Chicken vindaloo follows the same paste and method as the pork version but takes half the time to braise. Boneless thighs are the right cut: they remain moist throughout the long simmer, absorbing the vinegar paste without drying out the way breast meat does. The finished dish is sticky, deeply spiced, and sour, the flavour that defines vindaloo regardless of the protein.

The recipe requires the same vindaloo paste as the pork version: dried chillies or chilli flakes ground with garlic, ginger, vinegar, and whole spices. Make the paste and marinate for at least two hours. The braise itself takes only 35 to 40 minutes, enough time for the chicken to become tender and the sauce to reduce and coat.

items.name: Chicken thigh
items.prepNote: boneless, skinless, cut into 3 cm pieces

items.name: Chilli flakes
items.prepNote: reduce to 1 tsp for less heat

content.text: Toast {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds, the broken {{cinnamon-stick}}, the seeds from {{cardamom-pods}} cardamom pods, {{cloves}} cloves, and {{black-pepper}} peppercorns in a dry pan for 2 minutes until fragrant. Grind to a powder. Blend with {{garlic}} garlic cloves, {{ginger-fresh}} ginger, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli, {{turmeric-ground}} of turmeric, and {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar to a smooth paste. Toss {{chicken-thigh}} of chicken pieces in the paste with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours.

Heat {{sunflower-oil}} of oil in a Dutch oven over a medium heat. Add {{onion}} sliced onions and cook for 10 minutes until golden. Add the marinated chicken with all the paste. Stir and cook for 5 minutes. Pour in 150 ml of water, bring to the boil, cover, and reduce to a low simmer. Cook for 30 to 35 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the sauce is thick. Stir occasionally and add a splash of water if the sauce is catching. Rest for 5 minutes and serve with rice or flatbread.

items.fix: Use boneless thighs only. Check at 30 minutes, the chicken is done when it pulls apart easily with two forks.
items.cause: Breast meat used, or thighs overcooked

items.fix: Uncover for the last 10 minutes and simmer to reduce. The sauce should be thick enough to coat the chicken.
items.cause: Too much water added, or lid too tight

items.fix: Serve with plain yoghurt and rice. Next time start with 1 teaspoon of chilli flakes in the paste.
items.cause: Chilli quantity too much for this batch

content.text: Chicken vindaloo grew up alongside the pork original as a version for people who do not eat pork. It became standard on British Indian restaurant menus from the 1970s onwards. In Goa today both pork and chicken versions are common, though the pork dish stays the original and the more meaningful one. The chicken version has a much shorter cook time and is the more workable option on a weeknight.
